3 min read
Just now

--

“Compared to what?”
It’s a simple question.
Yet it’s responsible for preventing countless bad decisions in data science.
Imagine someone proudly walks into a meeting and announces:
“Our new machine learning model achieved 92% accuracy.”
Sounds impressive, right?
Maybe.
Maybe not.
Because the first question every data scientist should ask isn’t “How did you build it?”
It’s “Compared to what?”
That “what” is your baseline.
Without it, a model with 92% accuracy might be brilliant… or completely useless.
What Is a Baseline Model?
A baseline model is the simplest reasonable solution to a problem.
It isn’t designed to be smart.
It isn’t designed to impress anyone.
Its only job is to give you something to beat.
Think of it as setting the bar before you try to jump over it.
If your fancy machine learning model can’t outperform something incredibly simple, then all that extra complexity probably wasn’t worth it.
A Simple Example
Imagine you’re trying to predict whether a customer will cancel their subscription.
You spend days cleaning the data, engineering features, tuning hyperparameters, and training a sophisticated model.
Eventually, it reaches 85% accuracy.
Success?
Not so fast.
Now imagine someone builds a “model” that simply predicts every customer will stay.
No machine learning.
No feature engineering.
Nothing.
If 88% of customers actually stay, that simple prediction already achieves 88% accuracy.
Your expensive model scored 85%.
The baseline scored 88%.
The baseline wins.
Suddenly, your impressive model doesn’t look so impressive anymore.
Why Beginners Skip This Step
When you’re learning machine learning, it’s tempting to jump straight into the exciting part.
Random Forests.
XGBoost.
Neural Networks.
Deep Learning.
They’re fascinating.
But there’s a hidden trap.
Many beginners spend hours making increasingly complicated models without ever asking whether those models are actually better than the simplest possible solution.
Complexity isn’t progress.
Improvement is.
A Baseline Keeps You Honest
Baseline models have a way of cutting through excitement.
They don’t care how long you spent training your model.
They don’t care how many libraries you used.
They don’t care whether your notebook contains 500 lines of code.
They simply ask:
“Did you actually improve anything?”
That’s a question worth answering before celebrating any result.
Common Baselines
Depending on the problem, your baseline might be surprisingly simple.
For classification:

• Always predict the majority class.

• Predict randomly.

• Use a simple Logistic Regression model.
For regression:

• Always predict the average value.

• Predict the median.

• Use a simple Linear Regression model.
These models aren’t meant to be your final solution.
They’re your starting point.
The Real Goal Isn’t Accuracy
One of the biggest lessons in data science is realizing that numbers rarely speak for themselves.
An accuracy score has no meaning without context.
An RMSE value means nothing unless it’s compared to another model.
Even a breakthrough model isn’t really a breakthrough until it clearly outperforms the baseline.
That’s why experienced data scientists don’t celebrate good scores.
They celebrate better scores.
Final Thoughts
There’s a famous engineering idea that says:
“Everything should be made as simple as possible, but not simpler.”
Baseline models follow the same philosophy.
They remind us that the goal isn’t to build the most complicated model.
It’s to build the model that solves the problem better than what came before.
So the next time someone tells you their model achieved 95% accuracy, don’t ask which algorithm they used first.
Ask the question that matters.
Compared to what?
